originalTitle: Man sentenced to death for murder of toddlers at Ugandan nursery
title: Man sentenced to death for murder of toddlers at Ugandan nursery
content: Christopher Okello Onyum had pleaded insanity but this was dismissed by the judge.

originalTitle: 'How are we going to get back home?' Islamist group tightens blockade on Mali capital
title: Helping Hands for Our Friends in Mali!
content:
Hello from Africa! Let's Talk About Mali

Hello, amazing young friends from all over Africa and beyond! Your friendly kid-reporter is here with some news from our big, beautiful continent. Today, we're going to talk about a country called Mali. Can you find Mali on a map? It's a very special place in West Africa, known for its warm people, amazing music, and golden sands. Mali is a country with a long history, full of brave kings and queens, and ancient cities that taught the whole world about learning. Imagine cities made of mud and sand that are super old and still standing! That's Mali! Did you know? Mali is home to the mighty Niger River, which is like a giant blue ribbon flowing through the country, helping people grow food and travel. It's a bit like a super-highway for boats!

What's Happening in Bamako?

Every country has a main city, right? It's like the heart of the country, where many people live and important things happen. For Mali, that special city is called Bamako. It's a bustling place, full of markets, happy chatter, and the sounds of daily life. Right now, some grown-ups in Bamako are feeling a little worried. There's a group of people who are not being very kind, and they are trying to make it hard for things to get into the city. It's like they're trying to stop trucks with food and other important things from coming in. Imagine if you were waiting for a special delivery, like new crayons or your favorite snack, but the road was blocked. That's a little bit like what's happening, but for really important things like food and medicine.

Imagine a Big Roadblock!

Let's talk about what a 'blockade' means, in a super simple way. Imagine you have a toy train, and you want it to go all the way around your room. But then, someone puts a big pile of blocks in the way! The train can't get through, right? Well, that's what a blockade is, but for big trucks and roads. A group of people has put big 'blocks' on the roads that lead to Bamako. This means that big trucks carrying yummy food, helpful medicines, and other things that families need are having a hard time getting into the city. So, if you live in Bamako, it might be harder to find fresh vegetables or rice. It might be harder to get to the doctor if you're not feeling well because medicines are not arriving as easily. It's a big challenge for everyone. Did you know? Mali is famous for its delicious mangoes! Imagine if it was hard to get those sweet mangoes to everyone who wanted them because of a roadblock!

Why Is This Happening?

This big roadblock started happening after something very sad took place. A very important person, who was a helper for the country, was hurt very badly. We don't need to know all the grown-up details, but it made many people sad and worried. After that sad event, this group of people decided to make things difficult. They are trying to cause trouble and make people scared. But we know that most people in Mali, and everywhere else, want peace and kindness, not trouble. It's important to remember that not everyone in Mali is involved in this. Most people just want to live peacefully, go to school, play with their friends, and help their families. Did you know? Mali is famous for its amazing musicians! They play beautiful instruments like the kora, which sounds like a harp. Music often brings people together and helps them feel strong, even when things are tough.

What Does This Mean for Families?

When trucks can't bring food and supplies easily, it can be very tough for families. Imagine if your mum or dad went to the market, but the shelves were a bit empty. Or if they needed some medicine for a cough, but it wasn't there. Kids might wonder, "How am I going to get back home?" This is because some roads are not safe to travel on right now. It means that visiting family or friends who live far away can be tricky. Families really want to make sure everyone is safe and can be together. This kind of situation makes everyone think about how precious peace is. When there is peace, everyone can go to school, play outside, and their families can work and get everything they need. Did you know? Children in Mali love to play games like 'amare,' which is similar to marbles, and soccer, just like kids everywhere! They also love to tell stories and sing songs.

People Are Helping Each Other!

Even when things are tough, people in Mali are incredibly strong and kind. Neighbors are helping neighbors. If someone has a little extra food, they share it. If someone needs help, their community comes together. Many grown-ups, like leaders and brave helpers, are working very, very hard to try and fix this problem. They are talking to each other and trying to find a way to make the roads safe again so that trucks can come and go, and everyone can feel safe. Countries around the world, our big African family, and other friends are also watching and sending their good wishes and help. Everyone wants to see peace and happiness return to Bamako and all of Mali. Did you know? The Great Mosque of Djenné in Mali is the largest mud-brick building in the world! It's an amazing structure, rebuilt by hand every year by the community. It shows how people in Mali work together beautifully.

Hope for Peace and Kindness

It's important to remember that even when there are problems, there is always hope. Many people are praying for Mali, hoping that the roads will open soon, and that everyone will be safe. What can we do as kids? We can send our kind thoughts to the children of Mali. We can learn about their beautiful country and understand that even though there are troubles, the people are strong and full of hope. We can also be extra kind to our own friends and family. When we are kind and peaceful in our own lives, it helps spread goodness all over the world. Every little act of kindness makes a big difference! Did you know? Timbuktu, one of Mali's ancient cities, was once a very important center for learning and trade. Scholars from all over the world came there to study books and share ideas many, many years ago!

Our African Family Cares

We are all part of one big African family. When one part of our family is facing a challenge, we all care deeply. We hope that the children in Bamako will soon be able to go to school safely, play with their friends, and that their families will have everything they need. Let's keep Mali in our hearts and wish for peace, safety, and kindness to shine brightly over Bamako and all of Mali. We know that with help, hope, and determination, good things can happen. Let's send a big cheer for peace to all our friends in Mali! Your friendly kid-reporter, signing off with a wish for a peaceful world for all kids!
summary: Some people are making it hard for food and supplies to reach Mali's capital city, Bamako, causing worry for families, but many people are working together to bring peace and help.
whyMatters: This is important because when roads are blocked, it means less food and medicine for families and makes it hard for kids to go to school or for people to travel safely, showing how much we need peace for everyone to live happily.

originalTitle: 'I did not expect it': Kenya's Sabastian Sawe welcomed home with jubilant celebrations
title: Kenya's Super Runner Sabastian Sawe: A Hero's Welcome Home!
content:
Hello, my super friends! It's your kid-friendly news reporter, here to tell you a very exciting story from our wonderful continent, Africa!

Imagine someone running so fast, for so, so long, that they become a real-life hero! That's exactly what happened to our friend, Sabastian Sawe, from Kenya. He is a super, super runner, and he did something absolutely amazing!

Sabastian ran a very, very long race called a 'marathon'. This race is so long, it's like running from your home to a far-away town and back again, many, many times! And guess what? Sabastian ran it super fast! Faster than almost anyone in a real competition. He finished the whole race in less than two hours! Can you believe it?

When Sabastian came back home to Kenya, everyone was so, so happy and proud. They gave him the biggest welcome party ever!

A Big Welcome Home!

When Sabastian arrived back in Kenya, the air was full of joy! Lots and lots of people came out to see him. There were children, mothers, fathers, grandmas, and grandpas! Everyone was smiling, clapping their hands, and cheering very loudly.

Imagine a big party just for you! That's how it felt for Sabastian. People waved colourful flags, danced to happy music, and sang joyful songs. They shouted, 'Welcome home, Sabastian!' and 'You are our champion!'

His family was there too, giving him big hugs and proud smiles. It was a very special day, full of love and happiness. You could feel the pride in the air, like a big, warm blanket wrapping around everyone. Sabastian looked so happy and a little surprised by all the love. He didn't expect such a huge welcome, but he loved it!

It showed how much everyone in Kenya appreciates his hard work and amazing talent. It wasn't just a welcome; it was a celebration of courage, speed, and making a whole country proud!

What is a Marathon?

Now, let's talk about this 'marathon' race. It sounds like a big word, right? A marathon is a very, very long running race. It's about 42 kilometres long!

To give you an idea, imagine running around your school playground not just once, or twice, but maybe over a hundred times! Or running from your village to another village that is very far away, without stopping!

It takes a lot of strength, patience, and practice to run a marathon. Runners train for many, many months, running long distances every day, just to get ready for this one big race. They eat healthy food and drink lots of water to keep their bodies strong.

Did you know? The idea of a marathon race comes from a very old story! A long, long time ago, a brave messenger ran a very far distance to share important news. Today, people run marathons all over the world to test their strength and determination!

Sabastian's Super Speed!

Sabastian Sawe ran this super long marathon in less than two hours! That is incredibly fast. Most people take three or even four hours to finish a marathon, or even longer! To run it in under two hours means he was flying like a superhero!

He was the very first person in a real, competitive race to run the marathon so quickly. This is a new world record! A record means he ran faster than anyone else who has ever run that particular race before. It's like being the fastest kid in your class, but for the whole wide world in this super long race!

Sabastian didn't just wake up one day and become super fast. He worked very, very hard. He spent many hours training, running in the morning, running in the evening. He pushed himself even when he was tired, because he had a big dream in his heart.

His amazing speed shows that with lots of practice and not giving up, you can achieve incredible things!

A Hero for Kenya!

Why is this so important for Kenya? Well, when Sabastian achieves something so amazing, it makes everyone in Kenya feel very proud. It's like when your friend wins a prize at school, and you feel happy for them and proud to be their friend. But imagine that feeling for a whole country!

Sabastian's victory shows the whole world how strong and talented Kenyan runners are. Kenya is known for having many amazing long-distance runners, and Sabastian has just added another shining star to that list.

He is a hero because he inspires other children, maybe even you! He shows that if you work hard and believe in yourself, you can achieve your dreams, no matter how big they are. He makes children think, 'Maybe I can be a super runner too!'

Did you know? Kenya is famous for having some of the fastest long-distance runners in the world! Many champions like Sabastian come from our beautiful country, training in the high lands where the air helps make them super strong!

Dreams Come True!

Sabastian himself said he didn't expect to run so fast. Sometimes, even heroes are surprised by their own strength! He trained hard, did his best, and amazing things happened.

This tells us a very important lesson: always try your best. Work hard for what you want, and never, ever give up. Sometimes, the results will be even better than you imagined!

Sabastian's story reminds us that dreams can come true. With dedication and a big heart, you can reach for the stars, just like our super runner, Sabastian Sawe.

What's Next for Our Hero?

Sabastian will surely keep running and inspiring us all. He will continue to train and maybe break even more records in the future. He has shown us that with focus and determination, the sky is the limit!

And Kenya will continue to cheer for him, always proud of our champions. So, next time you are running or playing, remember Sabastian Sawe and his super speed. Keep dreaming big, little runners!

That's all for now from your kid-friendly news reporter. Stay tuned for more amazing stories from Africa!
summary: Sabastian Sawe, a super runner from Kenya, ran a very long marathon race in less than two hours, making him a world hero, and he received a giant, happy welcome home from his proud country.
whyMatters: This is important because Sabastian Sawe showed the whole world how amazing Kenyan runners are, inspiring kids to work hard and believe that big dreams can come true if you never give up.

originalTitle: US Congress passes bill to resume funding for DHS and end partial shutdown
title: US Congress passes bill to resume funding for DHS and end partial shutdown
content: House approves funding measure, which excludes ICE, ending weeks-long crisis that left many federal workers without pay.

title: Uganda court sentences man to death for killing four children at nursery
content: Christopher Okello Onyum sentenced for fatal stabbing of four children aged between one and three years old.

title: Trump says Tennessee next to redistrict after US Voting Rights Act ruling
content: Ruling weakening Voting Rights Act opens door to more states redrawing maps, diluting voting power of minority groups.

title: Myanmar ex-leader Aung San Suu Kyi moved to house arrest, military says

originalTitle: Oil price hits highest since 2022 after report Trump to be briefed on new Iran options
title: Oil Prices and Big Plans Far Away!
content:
Hello, Super Friends of Africa!

Hello, hello, super-duper kids of Africa! It's your friendly news reporter here, bringing you big news in a way that's easy to understand. We talk about what's happening in our world, right? Today, we're going to talk about something important called 'oil' and some grown-up discussions happening very far away. Don't worry, we'll keep it simple and fun!

What is Oil, Really?

Imagine finding sticky, dark brown, thick goo deep, deep underground. That's oil! It's not the kind of oil you cook with, but a special kind from the earth. This special goo was made over millions and millions of years from tiny plants and animals that lived long, long ago. It's like magic from history! *Did you know?* Some people call oil 'black gold' because it is so very valuable and important for many things we use every day! It's like a super energy drink for many machines around the world. Without it, lots of things would stop!

Why Do We Need Oil in Africa?

Oil helps our cars, taxis, and big buses zoom along the roads! It makes them go from one town to another, taking us to school or market. It also helps make electricity so our lights turn on when it gets dark. Imagine a world without lights! Oil helps us avoid that. Many families here in Africa use special lamps or stoves that use oil for cooking or to light their homes. So, it's very useful! *Did you know?* Many of your favorite plastic toys, like building blocks or toy cars, are also made from oil! Even your school pens can be made from oil. So, oil helps us travel, learn, cook, and even play. It's a big part of how our world works.

What Does "Oil Price" Mean?

When you go to the shop to buy a yummy treat or a new schoolbook, it has a price, right? That's how much money you need to pay for it. Oil also has a price. It's how much money grown-ups pay to buy this special goo from the ground. Sometimes the price of oil goes up, and sometimes it goes down. It changes like the weather! When the price goes up, it means it costs more money to buy the oil. And when oil costs more, other things that use oil might cost more too.

Why Did the Oil Price Go Up Lately?

Okay, here's the news part! Lately, the price of oil went up. It went up quite a bit! This happened because of some important grown-up talks and ideas happening very far away. There are two countries we're talking about today. One is called Iran, and the other is called the USA. *Did you know?* Iran is a country in a part of the world called the Middle East. It's very, very far from our beautiful continent of Africa! *Did you know?* The USA is also very far away, across a big ocean! It's another big country in the world. These two countries, Iran and the USA, have been having some disagreements, a bit like when friends don't agree on something.

Talking About Big Plans

A group of grown-ups in the USA, like a team of super planners, are always thinking about how to keep their country safe. They made some ideas or 'plans'. These ideas are like when a coach plans strategies for a big soccer game. They are thinking about how to make sure everyone listens and follows important rules. Their plans are about sending a very strong message to Iran. They are called 'options' or 'plans for strong warnings'. It means they are thinking about different ways to solve the problem. *Did you know?* Grown-ups, especially leaders, often make many, many plans and talk for a long time before they decide what to do. It's all about thinking carefully! They want to show they are serious about their disagreements.

A Leader is Listening

In the USA, there is a very important leader. He is like the chief of the country, or a president. His name is Mr. Trump. Mr. Trump is going to hear all about these plans and ideas. He will listen very carefully to what his planners have suggested. Then, he will have to decide what he thinks is the best thing to do next. It's a very big job! It's a bit like when your parents or guardians talk about plans for a big family trip or a new project for the house. They listen to ideas before making a decision.

How Might This Affect Us Here in Africa?

When the price of oil goes up because of these big talks far away, it can make a few things a little bit harder for us too. Your parents might have to pay a tiny bit more money for bus tickets or to put fuel in their car. This means travel costs a bit more. Also, the food at the market might cost a little bit more. This is because trucks use oil to bring the food from the farms to the market. *Did you know?* Our continent, Africa, is super big and beautiful! Some countries in Africa actually have a lot of oil themselves, like Nigeria or Angola! But even if oil comes from our own continent, the world's oil price still matters because we are all connected in the big world family.

What Happens Next?

Grown-ups and leaders all around the world are watching what happens. They are talking to each other to try and find a good, peaceful way for everyone to agree. We all hope that everyone can be friends and solve problems kindly, without any worries or big fights. *Did you know?* Many smart people are always working very hard to keep our world safe and fair for everyone, especially for children like you! They want to make sure everyone can live together happily and peacefully.

Keep Shining Bright!

So, don't you worry, super kids! Grown-ups are working hard to sort things out. It's good to know what's happening, but it's okay to keep playing and learning. The world is a big place, and we all share it. Let's always wish for peace, happiness, and kindness for everyone, everywhere. Thank you for listening to the news with me today. You are all amazing for wanting to learn about our world! Keep shining bright!
summary: Oil prices went up because big countries like Iran and the USA are having disagreements, and a leader named Mr. Trump is hearing ideas on what to do next to send a strong message.
whyMatters: When oil prices go up, things like bus rides and food might cost a little more for families, so grown-ups hope for peace to keep everything fair.

originalTitle: US House votes to end government shutdown over immigration operations
title: US House votes to end government shutdown over immigration operations
content: The vote provides funding for most Department of Homeland Security agencies, except for two subagencies responsible for immigration enforcement.

originalTitle: Iran miss Fifa congress but 'will play' at World Cup - Infantino
title: Iran miss Fifa congress but 'will play' at World Cup - Infantino
content: Fifa president Gianni Infantino says Iran will be at the 2026 World Cup, while Iranian officials are absent from the world football governing body's congress in Canada.
